**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Dr. S. Radhakrishnan:**
- Served as Indias **Ambassador to the USSR (**1949**"52)**.
- Later became the first Vice-President (**1952**"62) and second President (**1962**"67).
- **V.V. Giri:**
- Served as **High Commissioner to Ceylon (Sri Lanka)** in **1947**.
- Became Vice-President in **1967** and later the fourth President of India (**1969**"74).
- G.S. Pathak and B.D. Jatti were not diplomats before becoming Vice-President.
- K.R. Narayanan was indeed a diplomat, but he became Vice-President much later; the correct pair as per the question is (b).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The Chief Minister of a State in India is NOT eligible to vote in the Presidential election if
questions.questionText: The Chief Minister of a State in India is NOT eligible to vote in the Presidential election if

options.b: He is yet to prove his majority on the floor of the Lower House of the State legislature
options.c: He is a member of the Upper House of the State legislature
options.d: He is a care taker Chief Minister

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Article 54:** The Electoral College for Presidential elections consists of:
- Elected members of both Houses of Parliament, and
- **Elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States**.
- **Members of Legislative Councils (Upper House) do not vote** in Presidential elections.
- Therefore, if the **Chief Minister is a member of the Legislative Council, he is not part of the Electoral College**, hence cannot vote.
- Whether he is caretaker or has to prove majority has no effect on eligibility.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The only instance when the President of India exercised his power of veto related to
questions.questionText: The only instance when the President of India exercised his power of veto related to
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The Presidents veto powers are of three types:
- **Absolute Veto** " refusal to assent to the bill.
- **Suspensive Veto** " returning a non-money bill for reconsideration.
- **Pocket Veto** " taking no action.
- In **1954**, President Dr. Rajendra Prasad used his **absolute veto** to withhold assent to the **PEPSU (Patiala and East Punjab States Union) Appropriation Bill**.
- Reason: The bill was passed after Presidents Rule ended, so it was no longer valid.
- This is the only instance of the President using the absolute veto.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: The abolition of the I.A.S. and the I.P.S. has been recommended by the
questions.questionText: The abolition of the I.A.S. and the I.P.S. has been recommended by the
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- The **Rajamannar Committee** was set up by the DMK Government of Tamil Nadu in **1969** under the chairmanship of Dr. P.V. Rajamannar.
- Purpose: Examine **Centre"State relations** and recommend steps to make the federation more balanced.
- The Committee submitted its report in **1971**, making radical recommendations such as:
- **Abolition of All India Services (IAS, IPS, IFS)**, arguing they represented excessive central control over states.
- Establishment of an Inter-State Council with real powers.
- Curtailment of the Unions power to dismiss State governments.
- The IAS and IPS were retained, but this recommendation highlighted state-level discontent with central dominance.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: A High Court can withdraw a case from a subordinate court and can deal with the case itself if it is satisfied that the case involves a substantial point of constitutional law.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
1. 1 and 2
2. 1 and 3
3. 1 and 4
questions.questionText: A High Court can withdraw a case from a subordinate court and can deal with the case itself if it is satisfied that the case involves a substantial point of constitutional law. Select the correct answer using the codes given below: 1. 1 and 2 2. 1 and 3 3. 1 and 4 4. 2, 3 and 4
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- Under **Article 228**, a High Court may withdraw a case from a subordinate court if:
- It involves a **substantial question of constitutional law**.
- The High Court may either **dispose of the case itself** or **decide the constitutional question and send the case back**.
- The High Courts decision on constitutional matters is binding on subordinate courts in its jurisdiction.
- This provision ensures uniform interpretation of the Constitution within the state.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The Constitution of India borrowed the scheme of Indian Federation from the Constitution of
questions.questionText: The Constitution of India borrowed the scheme of Indian Federation from the Constitution of
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The Indian Constitution borrowed features from many constitutions:
- **British** ' Parliamentary system, Rule of law
- **US** ' Fundamental rights, judicial review
- **Irish** ' DPSPs, Presidential election method
- **Canadian** ' **Federal scheme with a strong Centre**, residuary powers with Union, Governor appointed by Centre, advisory jurisdiction of SC.
- Article 1 describes India as a Union of States, indicating **asymmetrical federalism like Canada** " some states have special status.
- Thus, Indias federal structure resembles Canadas more than the USAs.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: State Legislatures Types, Committees, Procedures, Bills, etc.
statementLines: Which one of the following correctly reflects the position of the Upper House of the State Legislature as compared to the position of the Upper House of the Parliament?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following correctly reflects the position of the Upper House of the State Legislature as compared to the position of the Upper House of the Parliament?

options.a: While one-third of the members of the Rajya Sabha retire every second year, one-fourth of the members of the Legislative Council retire every eighteen months
options.b: There is no provision for a joint sitting of the Legislative Assembly and the Legislative Council in the States for resolving deadlocks while there is such a provision in the case of the two Houses of Parliament
options.c: While no Bill other than a Money Bill can originate in the State Legislative Council, no Bill can originate in the Rajya Sabha
options.d: While Rajya Sabha has twelve nominated members, the State Legislative Council has none

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- Like the Rajya Sabha, the Legislative Council is a permanent body, not subject to dissolution. One-third of its members retire every two years.
- **Key difference:**
- There is **no provision for joint sitting of the Legislative Assembly and Council to resolve a deadlock**.
- At the Centre, **Article 108 provides for a joint sitting of Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha** to resolve differences on ordinary bills.
- A bill rejected by the Assembly ends; the Council cannot override it. This shows the Council is weaker than the Rajya Sabha.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Which one of the following is not in the State List under the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not in the State List under the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The **Seventh Schedule** divides subjects into Union List (List I), State List (List II), and Concurrent List (List III).
- **State List** includes subjects like public order, police, public health, agriculture, fisheries, markets, betting and gambling.
- **Insurance falls under the Union List**, giving Parliament the exclusive power to legislate on it.
- This arrangement ensures economic and financial subjects of national importance remain under Union control.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: The introduction of Pre-natal Diagnostic Techniques Bill in Parliament was prompted by the increasing incidence of
questions.questionText: The introduction of Pre-natal Diagnostic Techniques Bill in Parliament was prompted by the increasing incidence of

options.c: Spurious drugs being used for prenatal immunisation
options.d: Unqualified persons diagnosing pregnancy in villages by primitive methods

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- Amniocentesis and similar prenatal diagnostic techniques were widely misused in the 1980s to determine the sex of the foetus, followed by **selective abortion of female foetuses**.
- This led to public outrage, activism, and debate across India.
- Consequently, Parliament enacted the **Pre-Natal Diagnostic Techniques (Regulation and Prevention of Misuse) Act in 1994**, effective from **1996**, later amended in **2003** to include pre-conception techniques.
- The Act **prohibits sex selection** before or after conception and regulates diagnostic techniques to prevent misuse.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following statements regarding the election of the President of India is correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements regarding the election of the President of India is correct?

options.a: A candidate securing the majority of votes is not automatically elected
options.b: The Supreme Court has no jurisdiction in any doubt or dispute arising in connection with the election of the President
options.c: The Presidential election cannot take place when one or more State Assemblies stand dissolved because all the State Assemblies form part of the electoral college
options.d: The total value of the votes allotted to both Houses of Parliament is much more than the total value of the votes of all the States taken together

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- The election of the President is governed by **Article 54** and the Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, **1952**.
- The election uses **proportional representation by means of single transferable vote**, and voting is by secret ballot.
- The value of votes of MPs is calculated by dividing the total value of votes of all elected MLAs by the number of elected MPs.
- The **combined value of votes of Parliament is indeed more than that of the States taken together**. This ensures a federal balance tilted slightly towards the Union.
- A candidate must secure a quota (total valid votes 2 + 1) to be elected. Merely securing a majority of first preference votes doesnt guarantee automatic election if the quota isnt met.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
statementLines: Holding of elections for the Panchayats is decided by
questions.questionText: Holding of elections for the Panchayats is decided by
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992** inserted Part IX (Articles 243"243O) into the Constitution, giving constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions.
- **Article 243K:**
- The superintendence, direction, and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of Panchayat elections is vested in the **State Election Commission (SEC)**.
- The SEC is headed by a State Election Commissioner, appointed by the Governor, and enjoys security of tenure similar to High Court judges.
- Neither the District Magistrate nor the State Government conducts these elections.
- The Election Commission of India conducts elections for Parliament and State Legislatures, not Panchayats.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Assertion (A): Equality before the law is not applicable to the President of India Reason (R): The President of India enjoys special powers and privileges under the Constitution
questions.questionText: Assertion (A): Equality before the law is not applicable to the President of India Reason (R): The President of India enjoys special powers and privileges under the Constitution

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Article 14** guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws. However, this is not absolute " the **Constitution itself creates exceptions**.
- The **President of India enjoys constitutional immunities under Article 361**:
- **No criminal proceedings** shall be instituted during their term.
- **No arrest or imprisonment** shall be issued against the President.
- **No answerability to any court** for acts done in the exercise of official powers.
- Civil proceedings can be initiated only after due notice.
- These privileges are to **protect the dignity and functioning** of the highest constitutional office, not to grant personal impunity.
- Thus, both A and R are true and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The value of a vote of a member of the Parliament for the election of the President of India is determined by dividing the
questions.questionText: The value of a vote of a member of the Parliament for the election of the President of India is determined by dividing the

options.a: Nations population as per the latest census by the number of Lok Sabha members
options.b: Population as per the latest census by the total strength of the two Houses of the Parliament
options.c: The total value of votes of the members of all the State Legislative Assemblies by the elected members of the two Houses of the Parliament
options.d: Particular States population as per the latest census by the number of members of Parliament elected from that State

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Article 55** of the Constitution lays down the procedure for calculating the value of votes for Presidential elections:
- The value of votes of MLAs = (Population of the state / Number of elected MLAs) 1000.
- The **value of votes of MPs** = **Total value of votes of all MLAs of all States Total number of elected MPs (LS + RS)**.
- This ensures **uniformity in the scale of representation** of different states and parity between states and the Union.
- **Nominated MPs do not participate** in the Presidential election.
- The latest census used is the **1971** Census, as the seat allocation and vote values have been frozen until **2026**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding a No-confidence Motion in the Parliament:
1. There is no mention of a No-confidence Motion in the Constitution of India.
2. A period of six months must elapse between the introduction of one No-confidence Motion and the other.
3. At least 100 members must support a Motion of No-confidence before it is introduced in the House.
4. A motion of No-confidence can be introduced in the Lok Sabha only.
Of these statements:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding a No-confidence Motion in the Parliament: 1. There is no mention of a No-confidence Motion in the Constitution of India. 2. A period of six months must elapse between the introduction of one No-confidence Motion and the other. 3. At least 100 members must support a Motion of No-confidence before it is introduced in the House. 4. A motion of No-confidence can be introduced in the Lok Sabha only. Of these statements:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- **Statement 1: Correct.** The **Constitution does not mention** No-confidence motions. It is a parliamentary practice derived from Rules of Procedure (Rule 198, Lok Sabha).
- **Statement 2: Incorrect.** There is **no such restriction of 6 months** between two no-confidence motions.
- **Statement 3: Incorrect.** Only **50 members** must rise in support for it to be admitted, not 100.
- **Statement 4: Correct.** A No-confidence Motion can be introduced **only in Lok Sabha**, since the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha (Article 75).
- If passed by a simple majority, the Council of Ministers must resign.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
statementLines: Assertion (A): Panchayati Raj institutions were set up to decentralise planning to village level.
Reason (R): Village panchayats are in a better position to have a proper appreciation of their developmental needs.
questions.questionText: Assertion (A): Panchayati Raj institutions were set up to decentralise planning to village level. Reason (R): Village panchayats are in a better position to have a proper appreciation of their developmental needs.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Assertion (A): True.** The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992** gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) to ensure **decentralised governance and participatory democracy**.
- **Reason (R): True.** Village Panchayats directly engage with people and can **better assess local needs, resources, and developmental priorities** compared to distant centralised bodies.
- The Act included:
- Three-tier system (village, block, district),
- Reservation of seats for SCs/STs/Women,
- 11th Schedule with 29 subjects for Panchayats,
- Establishment of State Election Commissions and State Finance Commissions.
- Thus, both statements are true and R explains A correctly.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Parliament or a State Legislature can declare a seat vacant if a member abstains himself without permission from the sessions for at least
questions.questionText: Parliament or a State Legislature can declare a seat vacant if a member abstains himself without permission from the sessions for at least
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Article 101(4)** (for Parliament) and **Article 190(4)** (for State Legislatures):
- If a member is **absent from all meetings for 60 days or more**, without permission, his seat may be declared vacant.
- The period of sixty days **excludes any period when the House is prorogued or adjourned for more than four consecutive days**.
- This provision ensures accountability and discourages absenteeism among elected representatives.
- Similar provisions are included in the Rules of Procedure of both Houses.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XV: Elections
statementLines: Which among the following is not a part of electoral reforms?
questions.questionText: Which among the following is not a part of electoral reforms?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- Electoral reforms in India aim to make elections free, fair, and transparent.
- Some key reforms:
- **Registration of political parties** under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, **1951**.
- **Disqualification of candidates** involved in electoral malpractices or criminal offences.
- **Introduction of Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs)** in **1998** to curb booth capturing and improve efficiency.
- The **appointment of Election Commissioners** is governed by **Article 324** of the Constitution and is not itself an electoral reform " it's a **constitutional mechanism**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: State Legislatures Types, Committees, Procedures, Bills, etc.
statementLines: No money bill can be introduced in the legislative assembly without the recommendations of the
questions.questionText: No money bill can be introduced in the legislative assembly without the recommendations of the
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- Under **Article 207**, a Money Bill can be introduced only in the Legislative Assembly, not in the Legislative Council (if present), and **only on the recommendation of the Governor**.
- Every Money Bill is considered a **Government Bill** and can only be introduced by a Minister.
- Once passed by the Assembly, the Bill is sent to the Council (if applicable), which has limited powers " it cannot reject or amend, only recommend, and must **return it within 14 days**.
- Ultimately, the Legislative Assembly has overriding powers regarding a Money Bill.
- The Governor can assent, withhold assent, or reserve the bill for President, but **cannot return it for reconsideration**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Cognizable offence refers to an offence where
questions.questionText: Cognizable offence refers to an offence where

options.a: Arrests can be made without warrant.
options.b: Arrests can be made with warrant.
options.c: It is under the jurisdiction of a court.
options.d: Police can register a case without formal complaint.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- **Section 2(c) of CrPC (**1973**):**
- **Cognizable offences** are those where police can **arrest without warrant** and start investigation without prior approval of a magistrate.
- Examples: murder, rape, kidnapping, dowry death, theft, etc.
- Police can **register FIRs suo motu**, i.e., even without a formal complaint.
- Non-cognizable offences require a warrant and magistrates permission for investigation.
- Option (a) is partially correct, but option (d) captures the core legal definition more precisely.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: The first Leader of the opposition to get recognition in the Indian Parliament was
questions.questionText: The first Leader of the opposition to get recognition in the Indian Parliament was
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- In India, the **Leader of the Opposition** is the leader of the largest Opposition party having **at least 1/10th of the total seats** in the House.
- The position was formally recognised in **1969**.
- **Ram Subhag Singh**, a senior Congress leader from Bihar who later joined Congress (O), was recognised as the **first official Leader of the Opposition** in the Lok Sabha in **1969**.
- Later, Y. B. Chavan (**1977**"**1979**) became one of the most prominent Leaders of Opposition.
- The Leader of Opposition was given **statutory status in 1977**, entitling them to salary, allowances and facilities equivalent to a Cabinet Minister.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Absolute majority means _____ per cent of votes.
questions.questionText: Absolute majority means _____ per cent of votes.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Absolute majority** means **more than 50% of the total membership** of a House " not just those present and voting.
- **Example:**
- If the total strength of the Lok Sabha is 545, then **absolute majority = 273 (50% + 1)**.
- This majority is usually required for:
- Formation of government in Lok Sabha.
- No-confidence motions.
- Removal of Speaker or Deputy Speaker.
- It is different from:
- **Effective majority** ' majority of effective strength (total strength " vacancies).
- **Special majority** ' used for constitutional amendments, varies by article.
- **Simple majority** ' more than 50% of members present and voting.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Assertion (A): The Indian Constitution closely follows the British Parliamentary model.
Reason (R): In India the Upper House of the Parliament has judicial powers.
questions.questionText: Assertion (A): The Indian Constitution closely follows the British Parliamentary model. Reason (R): In India the Upper House of the Parliament has judicial powers.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Assertion (A) is true:** India adopted the **British Parliamentary System**, characterised by:
- Nominal and real executives,
- Collective responsibility of the executive,
- Leadership of PM,
- Fusion of powers.
- **Reason (R) is false:** The **Rajya Sabha (Upper House) does not have judicial powers**; only the Judiciary has that role.
- Rajya Sabha has legislative, deliberative, and some special powers (e.g., Art. 249), but **no judicial functions**.
- Therefore, while the assertion is correct, the reason is wrong.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: It is for any economic consideration involving loss of freedom of contract leading to forced employment.
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below:
2. 1 and 2
3. 1 and 3
4. 2 and 3
questions.questionText: It is for any economic consideration involving loss of freedom of contract leading to forced employment. Choose the correct answer from the codes given below: 1. 1, 2 and 3 2. 1 and 2 3. 1 and 3 4. 2 and 3
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Article 23** of the Constitution prohibits traffic in human beings, **begar (forced labour)** and other similar forms of forced labour.
- The term **begar** means compulsory work without remuneration, historically prevalent under feudal systems.
- The prohibition includes **economic compulsion** " forcing someone to work for less than minimum wage amounts to forced labour.
- The **Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act** further reinforced this prohibition by abolishing bonded labour agreements.
- Any contravention is punishable by law.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Who among the following have a right to vote in the Vice-Presidential election but not in the Presidential election?
questions.questionText: Who among the following have a right to vote in the Vice-Presidential election but not in the Presidential election?

options.a: Ministers who are not members of either House of the Parliament
options.b: Elected members of the Upper Houses of the States
options.d: Members of the Parliament who abstain from all meetings of the House for a period of sixty days without permission of the House

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Vice-President** is elected by an Electoral College consisting of members of **both Houses of Parliament (Article 66)**.
- It includes **elected as well as nominated members** of Parliament.
- **President** is elected by **elected MPs + elected MLAs** of all states and UTs with legislatures (**Article 54**).
- **Nominated members do not vote** in the Presidential election.
- So, **nominated members of Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha** have the right to vote for Vice-President, but not for President.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: The allocation of seats for each State in the present Lok Sabha is based on the
questions.questionText: The allocation of seats for each State in the present Lok Sabha is based on the
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Article 82** provides for readjustment of Lok Sabha seats after every Census through a **Delimitation Act**.
- The **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act,**1976**froze the allocation of Lok Sabha seats** to the states based on the **1971 Census**, to encourage population control measures.
- This freeze was extended by the **84th Amendment Act (**2001**) up to 2026**.
- The **87th Amendment** allowed delimitation of constituencies using the **2001** Census, but **without altering the number of seats per state**.
- Thus, the **1971 Census remains the basis** for the allocation of seats in Lok Sabha.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: The Constitution of India provides for the nomination of two members to the Lok Sabha by the President to represent
questions.questionText: The Constitution of India provides for the nomination of two members to the Lok Sabha by the President to represent

options.a: Men of eminence in arts, letters, science, etc.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- **Article 331** of the Constitution empowered the **President to nominate up to two members of the Anglo-Indian community** to the Lok Sabha if he/she is of the opinion that the community is not adequately represented.
- The aim was to safeguard the interests of the **Anglo-Indian community**, a small minority.
- Originally, the Lok Sabha could have 530 elected members from states, 20 from UTs, and **2 nominated Anglo-Indian members**.
-

Note: The **104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019** abolished this nomination from January 25, **2020**.
- Eminent persons are nominated to Rajya Sabha under Article 80.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: A political party is recognised as a national party
questions.questionText: A political party is recognised as a national party

options.a: When it contests elections in all the States of the country.
options.b: When it secures at least five per cent of the total votes cast in a national election.
options.c: If it captures power at least in three States.
options.d: If it is recognised as a political party in four or more States.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- As per the **Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968**, issued by the **Election Commission of India**, a political party can be recognised as a national party if it satisfies any of the following conditions:
- It wins **2% of seats in the Lok Sabha** (i.e., at least 11 seats) from **3 different States**, or-It secures **6% of valid votes in four or more States** in a Lok Sabha or Assembly election and wins **4 Lok Sabha seats**, or-It is **recognised as a State party in four or more States**.
- Recognition gives parties privileges like **reserved symbols**, free airtime on media, consultation in setting election dates, etc.
- Contesting in all states is not mandatory. Capturing power in three states is not a criterion.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: First non-Congress government to come in power in any Indian State is
questions.questionText: First non-Congress government to come in power in any Indian State is

options.b: Communist Party of India in Kerala

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- In **1957**, the **Communist Party of India (CPI)** won the **Kerala Assembly elections**, forming the **first non-Congress state government** in India.
- **E.M.S. Namboodiripad** became the first Communist Chief Minister.
- This was historic because:
- It was the **first democratically elected Communist government** in the world.
- It marked the **end of Congresss monopoly** in state politics.
- DMK came to power in Tamil Nadu only in **1967**.
- CPI(M) in West Bengal came later, in **1977**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: The maximum gap between two sessions of the Parliament can be of
questions.questionText: The maximum gap between two sessions of the Parliament can be of
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Article 85:** The **President summons** each House of Parliament from time to time, but **six months must not intervene** between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session.
- This ensures **regular parliamentary oversight** on the executive.
- Parliament usually meets in **three sessions each year**:
- **Budget Session** (Feb"May)
- **Monsoon Session** (July"Aug)
- **Winter Session** (Nov"Dec)
- A gap of more than six months would violate the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Parliament can legislate matters in the State List
questions.questionText: Parliament can legislate matters in the State List

options.b: If Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by 2/3rd majority
options.d: By asking the legislature of the State concerned

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Article 249:** Parliament can legislate on State List matters if the **Rajya Sabha**, by a **2/3rd majority of members present and voting**, declares it is necessary in the national interest.
- The resolution remains valid for **one year**, renewable annually.
- Other circumstances where Parliament can legislate on State List:
- **Article 250:** During a **National Emergency**.
- **Article 252:** When **two or more States** pass resolutions requesting Parliament to legislate.
- **Article 253:** To implement **international treaties**.
- **Article 356:** During **Presidents Rule** in a State.
- It is not dependent on the Presidents wish, nor can it be done under any circumstances.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Judges of a High Court are appointed by the President with the consent of the
questions.questionText: Judges of a High Court are appointed by the President with the consent of the

options.b: Governor, the Chief Justice of India and the Chief Justice of the High Court concerned
options.c: Governor and the preceding Chief Justice of the High Court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Article 217:** The President appoints High Court judges after consultation with:
- The **Chief Justice of India**,
- The **Governor** of the concerned State, and-In the case of appointment of a judge (other than Chief Justice), the **Chief Justice of that High Court**.
- Chief Justice of a High Court is appointed after consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor.
- **Second Judges Case (**1993**):** Supreme Court held that CJ of Indias opinion has primacy.
- **Third Judges Case (**1998**):** Appointment must be through **collegium** (CJI + 2 senior-most SC judges).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: India has Parliamentary democracy system because
questions.questionText: India has Parliamentary democracy system because

options.a: Members of the Lok Sabha are elected by the public
options.b: Council of Ministers is responsible to the Legislature
options.c: Of distribution of power between the Centre and the States

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- India has adopted the **Parliamentary system** of government, both at the Centre (Articles 74"75) and in the States (Articles 163"164).
- The key feature of the parliamentary system is **collective responsibility of the executive to the legislature**:
- The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the **Lok Sabha**.
- If the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion, the entire council resigns.
- Real executive is the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister, while the President is nominal executive.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: State Legislatures Types, Committees, Procedures, Bills, etc.
statementLines: The Speaker of the lower house of a State can step down by submitting his resignation to the
questions.questionText: The Speaker of the lower house of a State can step down by submitting his resignation to the
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The **Speaker of a State Legislative Assembly** holds office until the House is dissolved or he resigns or is removed by a resolution of the Assembly (Article 178"181).
- He may resign by writing to the **Deputy Speaker** (similar to the Lok Sabha Speaker who resigns to the Deputy Speaker).
- Removal requires a majority of all the then members of the House with 14 days notice.
- The Speaker does not resign to the Governor or CM.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Governor of which State has been entrusted with the special powers for the administration of tribal areas?
questions.questionText: Governor of which State has been entrusted with the special powers for the administration of tribal areas?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Sixth Schedule** of the Constitution contains special provisions for the administration of tribal areas in **Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram**.
- In these states, certain tribal areas are designated as **Autonomous Districts** with District and Regional Councils, enjoying legislative, administrative and judicial powers.
- The Governor of Assam plays a key role:
- He can organise and reorganise autonomous districts, alter their boundaries, or change names.
- He also has oversight powers over laws passed by District Councils.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: India is a federal state because of
questions.questionText: India is a federal state because of

options.c: Share of power between the Centre and the States

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Federalism** is characterised by **division of powers** between two levels of government " Central and State " each constitutionally independent in its sphere.
- In India:
- Legislative powers are divided into Union, State, and Concurrent Lists (**Seventh Schedule**).
- Executive and financial powers are similarly divided.
- There are independent constitutional mechanisms (e.g., Finance Commission, Inter-State Council) to manage this division.
- Dual citizenship does not exist in India (unlike the USA).
- Dual judiciary isnt an accurate feature (India has a single integrated judiciary).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: The Institute of Ombudsman was first established in
questions.questionText: The Institute of Ombudsman was first established in
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The word Ombudsman is of **Swedish origin**, meaning grievance person.
- The modern concept originated in **Sweden in 1809**, when the Swedish Parliament established the office of Justitieombudsman to safeguard citizens against administrative injustices.
- Functions of the Swedish Ombudsman include:
- Addressing abuse of administrative discretion.
- Investigating maladministration, corruption, nepotism, and official discourtesy.
- The Ombudsman system has since been adopted by many countries, including India in the form of **Lokpal and Lokayuktas** (Lokpal Act, **2013**).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: A Parliamentary bill passed by the Lok Sabha is sent to the President for assent. The President sends it back to Lok Sabha for reconsideration. If the Lok Sabha again sends it back to the President without making any changes, the President
questions.questionText: A Parliamentary bill passed by the Lok Sabha is sent to the President for assent. The President sends it back to Lok Sabha for reconsideration. If the Lok Sabha again sends it back to the President without making any changes, the President

options.a: Must give his assent to it
options.b: Can again send the bill for reconsideration
options.c: Can take help of the Supreme Court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Article 111** governs the Presidents powers regarding ordinary bills:
- The President may give assent, or withhold assent, or return the bill (if not a Money or Constitutional Amendment Bill) for reconsideration.
- If the bill is passed again by Parliament, with or without changes, and sent to the President the second time, the **President is constitutionally bound to give assent**.
- The President cannot return it again nor refer it to the Supreme Court nor call a referendum.
- This ensures that Parliaments will ultimately prevails and prevents a deadlock between the legislature and the executive.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following is a cognizable offence?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is a cognizable offence?

options.a: First offence committed by any person
options.d: Offence which can be dealt with by the court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- As per Section 2(c) of the Criminal Procedure Code, **1973**, **cognizable offences** are those where a police officer can **arrest without a warrant** and start investigation without the magistrates permission.
- These are serious offences, such as murder, rape, dowry death, kidnapping, theft, etc.
- In contrast, non-cognizable offences require a warrant and prior permission from a magistrate for investigation (e.g., defamation, public nuisance).
- The phrase dealt with by the court here refers to offences under regular judicial procedure, not private complaints.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
statementLines: Which of the following States are not covered by the Panchayati Raj?
questions.questionText: Which of the following States are not covered by the Panchayati Raj?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Meghalaya and Nagaland** are not covered by the **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**, which provides for the constitutional status of Panchayati Raj Institutions.
- This exclusion is due to their distinct tribal traditions, customary laws, and existing local governance systems protected under Sixth Schedule and Article 371A.
- **Sixth Schedule** provides for Autonomous District Councils, particularly in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
- Nagalands local self-government works through traditional tribal institutions.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Which of the following is correct about the Chief Election Commissioner? He holds the office till he attains the age of
questions.questionText: Which of the following is correct about the Chief Election Commissioner? He holds the office till he attains the age of

options.c: 62 years or completes 3 years of service, whichever is earlier
options.d: 65 years of age or completes 5 years of service, whichever is earlier

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The **Chief Election Commissioner (CEC)** and other Election Commissioners are appointed by the President under **Article 324**.
- They hold office for **6 years or until they attain the age of 65 years**, whichever is earlier.
- They enjoy **security of tenure** " removal only like a Supreme Court judge (by a special majority resolution of Parliament on grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity).
- Their conditions of service cannot be varied to their disadvantage during tenure.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: The Directive Principles of State Policy have been given precedence over Fundamental Rights in the.... Constitutional Amendment
questions.questionText: The Directive Principles of State Policy have been given precedence over Fundamental Rights in the.... Constitutional Amendment
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976** is also called the Mini Constitution because of its wide-ranging changes.
- It gave **Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV)** precedence over certain **Fundamental Rights (Part III)**, particularly those under Articles 14 and 19.
- A new clause was added to Article 31C, providing that no law implementing any of the Directive Principles could be declared invalid on the ground of violating Articles 14 or 19.
- Although the Supreme Court in Minerva Mills (**1980**) struck down the extension of Article 31C to all DPSPs, it upheld the basic precedence of Article 39(b) & (c) DPSPs over FRs.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Asom Gana Parishad was recently in the news because
questions.questionText: Asom Gana Parishad was recently in the news because

options.a: Of the 64 elected members, 52 are bachelors
options.b: All the 64 legislators are in the age group of 25 to 35
options.c: All the legislators have been elected for the first time

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- **Asom Gana Parishad (AGP)** emerged from the six-year-long Assam Movement (**1979**"**1985**) led by AASU & AAGSP, demanding detection and deportation of illegal migrants.
- After the **Assam Accord (**1985**)**, AGP contested the state elections in December **1985** and won a landslide, forming the government.
- Notable facts about AGP MLAs:
- Majority were young first-time legislators.
- Many were bachelors.
- All belonged to 25"35 age group, marking a generational shift in Assamese politics.
- Prafulla Kumar Mahanta, aged 33, became Indias youngest CM at the time.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Sarkaria Commission was set up to
questions.questionText: Sarkaria Commission was set up to

options.a: Investigate about the riots that took place in Delhi in 1984
options.c: Make necessary investigations and suggestions regarding the problem of Punjab
options.d: Investigate about the assassination of Mrs. Indira Gandhi

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Sarkaria Commission** was constituted in **1983** under Justice R.S. Sarkaria to review **Centre"State relations** and recommend reforms.
- Key recommendations:
- Set up a permanent **Inter-State Council (Article 263)** ' later implemented.
- Use Article 356 sparingly.
- Promote consultation with States before legislating on Concurrent List.
- Strengthen Zonal Councils.
- It was not related to Punjab, riots, or Indira Gandhis assassination.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: The main function of the National Development Council is
questions.questionText: The main function of the National Development Council is

options.c: To review the working of the National Plan from time to time

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **National Development Council (NDC)** was set up in August **1952** by an executive resolution, not by the Constitution.
- **Composition:** PM (Chairman), Union Ministers, Chief Ministers, Lt. Governors of UTs, and members of the Planning Commission.
- **Main functions:**
- Prescribe guidelines for the preparation of National Plan.
- Consider and approve the National Plan.
- Assess resources required for the Plan.
- Consider policy questions affecting national development.
- **Review the working of the Plan from time to time.**
- Recommend measures for achieving Plan targets.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Mandamus means
questions.questionText: Mandamus means

options.a: A writ issued by a superior court commanding that a specified thing be done
options.b: A legal term for a prisoners right to appear in person and be tried in court
options.c: A written public declaration of the intentions, opinions or motives of a sovereign or of a leader, party or body
options.d: The principle of hybridisation, discovered by Gregor Mendel which led to the improved breeds of plant and animal life

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Mandamus literally means We command.**
- It is issued by a higher court to a public authority, corporation, tribunal, or inferior court, directing them to perform a public/legal duty which they have failed or refused to perform.
- It is called a **wakening call** " it wakes up authorities from inaction.
- **Limitations:** It cannot be issued against private bodies, the President or Governors, Chief Justices acting in judicial capacity, to enforce non-statutory departmental instructions, or when duty is discretionary.
- It is one of the **five writs under Article 32 & 226**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: The most irritating factor in the Centre"State relationship is the
questions.questionText: The most irritating factor in the Centre"State relationship is the

options.b: Interference of the Centre in the functions allotted to them by the Constitution

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- River water disputes are generally interstate, not between Centre and States directly. The Centre usually acts as arbitrator (e.g., Cauvery, Krishna).
- Financial allocation is guided by the Finance Commission (Art. 280), a constitutional body, reducing scope for arbitrary interference.
- The real friction arises when the **Centre interferes in State matters**"especially through provisions like:
- **Article 356:** Imposition of Presidents Rule, often on political grounds.
- **Article 249 & 250:** Parliament legislating on State List matters in national interest or during emergencies.
- **Governors discretionary powers**, often seen as an agent of the Centre.
- These central interventions have historically been major irritants in Centre"State relations.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Who among the following is/are member(s) of Parliament?
1. Shivaji Ganesan
2. Amitabh Bachchan
3. Sunil Dutt
4. Vyjayanthimala
5. Jayalalitha
questions.questionText: Who among the following is/are member(s) of Parliament? 1. Shivaji Ganesan 2. Amitabh Bachchan 3. Sunil Dutt 4. Vyjayanthimala 5. Jayalalitha

options.b: B, C, D and E only
options.c: All of A, B, C, D and E
options.d: A, B, C and D only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Shivaji Ganesan** ' Nominated to Rajya Sabha in **1982** after the death of Nargis.
- **Amitabh Bachchan** ' Elected to Lok Sabha from Allahabad in **1984** on a Congress ticket with record victory margin.
- **Sunil Dutt** ' Elected to 8th Lok Sabha from Mumbai North-West on Congress ticket.
- **Vyjayanthimala** ' Elected twice to Lok Sabha and once nominated to Rajya Sabha.
- **Jayalalitha** ' Elected as Member of Parliament (Rajya Sabha) before becoming CM of Tamil Nadu.
All five have served in Parliament at some point.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: State Legislatures Types, Committees, Procedures, Bills, etc.
statementLines: What is the minimum percentage of the strength of opposition required in a State legislature for the appointment of an opposition leader?
questions.questionText: What is the minimum percentage of the strength of opposition required in a State legislature for the appointment of an opposition leader?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- There is **no specific constitutional or legal requirement** for a minimum percentage for appointment of a Leader of Opposition.
- However, by **parliamentary convention**, a party must secure at least **10% of the total strength of the House** to get official recognition as the Opposition Party and thereby have a Leader of the Opposition.
- If no party meets this criterion, no formal Leader of the Opposition is appointed.
- This rule applies both at the Union and State levels.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: The National Anthem was written by
questions.questionText: The National Anthem was written by
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Jana Gana Mana** was originally composed in Bengali by **Rabindranath Tagore**.
- It was first sung on 27 December **1911** at the Calcutta session of the Indian National Congress.
- It was adopted as the National Anthem by the Constituent Assembly on 24 January **1950**.
- The full song has five stanzas; the playing time of the full version is approximately 52 seconds.
- (b) Bankim Chandra wrote Vande Mataram, not Jana Gana Mana.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part VIII & X: Union Territories and Scheduled Areas
questions.microTopic: UT, Scheduled Areas, Other Scheduled Areas
statementLines: The Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains
questions.questionText: The Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains

options.b: Salaries of the President, Governors of States, Judges of Supreme Court, etc.
options.c: Union List, State List and Concurrent List
options.d: Allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Seventh Schedule** provides for three-fold distribution of legislative subjects between the Centre and the States:
- **List I " Union List** (currently 100 subjects): Parliament has exclusive power (e.g., defence, banking, foreign affairs).
- **List II " State List** (currently 61 subjects): States have exclusive power in normal times (e.g., police, public health, agriculture).
- **List III " Concurrent List** (currently 52 subjects): Both Parliament and States can legislate; in case of conflict, Parliaments law prevails.
- This division is inspired by the **Government of India Act, 1935** and ensures a federal legislative structure.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part VIII & X: Union Territories and Scheduled Areas
questions.microTopic: UT, Scheduled Areas, Other Scheduled Areas
statementLines: Which of the following languages are included in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which of the following languages are included in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The **Eighth Schedule** originally contained 14 languages, and over the years, more were added through Constitutional Amendments.
- **Sindhi** was added by the 21st Amendment Act, **1967**.
- **Kashmiri, Konkani and Nepali** were added by the 71st Amendment Act, **1992**.
- **English is not listed** in the Eighth Schedule " it's an official language, but not a scheduled language.
- Therefore, the correct combinations from the given options are (c) Kashmiri and Konkani, and (d) Sindhi and Kashmiri (

Note: question options presented allowed for multiple correct combinations in the raw file, outputting standard format).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
statementLines: The Panchayati Raj was first introduced in the States of
questions.questionText: The Panchayati Raj was first introduced in the States of
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (**1957**)** recommended a three-tier Panchayati Raj for grassroots democracy.
- **Inauguration:** 2 October **1959**, Nagaur (Rajasthan) by Jawaharlal Nehru.
- **Second state:** Andhra Pradesh, 9 days later at Shadnagar.
- **Constitutional status:** Later entrenched via the **73rd Constitutional Amendment (**1992**)**, adding Part IX & Eleventh Schedule.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: After retirement, a High Court Judge
questions.questionText: After retirement, a High Court Judge

options.a: Can plead in the same High Court
options.b: Cannot plead in the same High Court
options.c: Can plead in the Supreme Court
options.d: Can plead in any of the High Courts

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Article 220:** A permanent High Court judge, after retirement, shall not plead or act in the High Court in which he served (to avoid conflict of interest and maintain impartiality).
- **Permissible practice:** Such a retired judge may appear in the Supreme Court or in other High Courts (but not the same one).
- **Trap in options:** While (c) and (d) mention where they can practise, the question asks what is barred; hence the correct choice is the prohibition.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: In a Parliamentary System of Government
questions.questionText: In a Parliamentary System of Government

options.a: The parliament is responsible to the executive
options.b: The parliament is responsible to the judiciary
options.c: The executive is responsible to the parliament
options.d: The judiciary is responsible to the parliament

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- **Core doctrine:** Collective responsibility of the Council of Ministers to the Lok Sabha (**Article 75(3)**).
- **Practical control tools:** Question Hour, Zero Hour, censure & no-confidence motions, calling attention, adjournment motions, committee oversight.
- **Consequence:** If the ministry loses majority or a no-confidence motion passes, it must resign or seek dissolution.
- **Why others are wrong:** Parliament isnt responsible to the executive/judiciary; judiciary reviews legality, not policy.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: In which of the following States in India, the number of electorate is the highest?
questions.questionText: In which of the following States in India, the number of electorate is the highest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Electorate** means the total number of registered voters; it correlates strongly with population.
- **Uttar Pradesh is Indias most populous state**, therefore it has the largest voter list.
- Bihar, Rajasthan, Karnataka have smaller populations/electorates than U.P., so they trail behind.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The President of India is elected indirectly by the electoral college consisting of the elected members of the
questions.questionText: The President of India is elected indirectly by the electoral college consisting of the elected members of the

options.c: Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha only
options.d: Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha and the Legislative Assemblies of the States

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- **Electoral College:** Elected members of both Houses of Parliament + elected MLAs of all States and of NCT of Delhi & Puducherry.
- **Excluded:** Nominated members (both at Centre and States) do not vote in the Presidential election.
- **Voting system: Proportional Representation by Single Transferable Vote**; secret ballot.
- **Vote value equalisation:** Formula ensures uniformity among States and parity between States and the Union (**Article 55**).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Consider the following States with respect to their representation to the Lok Sabha:
1. Andhra Pradesh
2. Madhya Pradesh
3. Tamil Nadu
4. Maharashtra
Which of the following sequences is correct? (> means greater than)
questions.questionText: Consider the following States with respect to their representation to the Lok Sabha: 1. Andhra Pradesh 2. Madhya Pradesh 3. Tamil Nadu 4. Maharashtra Which of the following sequences is correct? (> means greater than)
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Allocation basis:** Seats are allotted broadly in proportion to population, and the numbers were frozen at **1971** census levels (42nd & 84th Amendments) till **2026**.
- **Seat counts (historical/frozen):** Maharashtra 48, Andhra Pradesh 42 (pre-bifurcation), Tamil Nadu 39, Madhya Pradesh 29 (pre-Chhattisgarh).
- **Why not others:** TN (39) is lower than AP (42), and MP (29) is lowest among the four, so any order putting MP before TN/AP is wrong.
- **Correct descending order:** Maharashtra (IV) > Andhra Pradesh (I) > Tamil Nadu (III) > Madhya Pradesh (II).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: Which is correct?
questions.questionText: Which is correct?

options.a: Lok Sabha cannot be dissolved before 5 years
options.b: Rajya Sabha lasts only for 6 years
options.c: Lok Sabha can be dissolved before 5 years
options.d: Life of Lok Sabha can be extended indefinitely

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The normal term of the **Lok Sabha is 5 years** from the date of its first sitting (**Article 83**).
- However, the **President can dissolve the Lok Sabha earlier** on the advice of the Council of Ministers, or if no government can be formed.
- During a national emergency, Parliament may extend the term of Lok Sabha by one year at a time, but not beyond 6 months after the emergency ends.
- The **Rajya Sabha, on the other hand, is a permanent house** and is not subject to dissolution; one-third of its members retire every 2 years.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Opposition conclave at Srinagar forwarded the following recommendations:
1. Government at the Centre should be shared by all parties
2. Governor should be appointed from a panel forwarded by the State Government concerned
3. Setting up of Inter-State Council to finalise disputes between the states
4. Giving more powers to the States
5. Articles 356 and 357 should be suitably amended
6. Resignation of Central Government Out of these only:
questions.questionText: Opposition conclave at Srinagar forwarded the following recommendations: 1. Government at the Centre should be shared by all parties 2. Governor should be appointed from a panel forwarded by the State Government concerned 3. Setting up of Inter-State Council to finalise disputes between the states 4. Giving more powers to the States 5. Articles 356 and 357 should be suitably amended 6. Resignation of Central Government Out of these only:

options.b: A, C and D are true
options.c: C, D, E and F are true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **Opposition Conclave at Srinagar (**1983**"84)** was held to highlight issues of **Centre-State relations** and the dominance of the Centre in Indian federalism.
- Key demands included:
- **Sharing power at the Centre** among all parties to prevent centralisation.
- **Appointment of Governors from a panel** suggested by the respective State Government to reduce bias.
- Other options like amendments to Articles 356/357 or creation of Inter-State Council were discussed later in different forums like the Sarkaria Commission.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XV: Elections
statementLines: Democracy exists in India. Government is run by the elected representatives of the people. For proper functioning of this system
questions.questionText: Democracy exists in India. Government is run by the elected representatives of the people. For proper functioning of this system

options.a: One should cast vote in elections
options.b: One must be prompt in paying taxes
options.c: One must be good to himself and to the society

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- Democracy functions effectively only when citizens actively participate in elections by exercising their **right to vote**.
- Voting enables people to choose their representatives, hold them accountable, and shape the direction of governance.
- While paying taxes and being socially responsible are important civic duties, **voting is the primary mechanism** through which democracy operates in a representative system like India.
- **Universal adult franchise** is the foundation of Indias democratic structure.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
statementLines: Panchayati Raj system was adopted to
questions.questionText: Panchayati Raj system was adopted to

options.d: To introduce a form of local self-government at the village, block and district levels

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The **Panchayati Raj system** was adopted to promote **decentralisation of power** and strengthen grassroots democracy.
- It aims to transfer decision-making powers from the Centre and States to local bodies, enabling **peoples participation in governance**.
- Based on the **Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (**1957**)** recommendations, the system was first implemented in **1959 in Rajasthan**.
- Later, the **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992** gave it constitutional status, mandating a three-tier structure " village, block, and district levels.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Who selects persons for appointment to the Central Civil Services in India?
questions.questionText: Who selects persons for appointment to the Central Civil Services in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- The **Union Public Service Commission (**UPSC**)**, established under **Articles 315"323**, is the central recruiting agency for appointments to the All India Services, Central Services, and services of Union Territories.
- It conducts various competitive examinations such as Civil Services Examination, Engineering Services, etc.
- While the **President makes the formal appointment**, the selection is carried out by UPSC, ensuring independence, meritocracy, and transparency in recruitment.
- **UPSC** is a **constitutional body**, independent of the executive.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Centre-State financial relations are looked after by the
questions.questionText: Centre-State financial relations are looked after by the
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **Finance Commission**, established under **Article 280**, is a constitutional body responsible for recommending the distribution of financial resources between the Union and the States.
- It is constituted by the **President every five years** or earlier if necessary.
- Its key functions include:
- Distribution of **net proceeds of taxes** between the Centre and States.
- Laying down the principles for **grants-in-aid to States**.
- Recommending measures to augment State finances.
- It plays a crucial role in maintaining **fiscal federalism** in India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part II: Citizenship
statementLines: A good citizen is one who is
questions.questionText: A good citizen is one who is
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- A good citizen is expected to be aware of and fulfil **social obligations** such as respecting laws, paying taxes, and participating actively in democratic processes.
- Citizenship involves both **rights and duties**; being socially conscious ensures harmony and collective progress.
- Merely being educated or culturally rich does not ensure responsible citizenship without active contribution to society.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Mandal Commission report pertains to
questions.questionText: Mandal Commission report pertains to
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **Mandal Commission** was set up in **1979** by the Janata Party Government under Prime Minister **Morarji Desai**, chaired by **Bindheshwari Prasad Mandal**.
- Its purpose was to identify **socially and educationally backward classes** and recommend measures for their advancement.
- It recommended **27% reservation** in government jobs for **Other Backward Classes (OBCs)**, so that total reservations for SCs, STs, and OBCs reached 50%.
- The recommendations were implemented in **1990** by the V.P. Singh Government, which led to major political and social changes.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Match the following:
1. Chief Election Commissioner - Elected by members of Rajya Sabha
2. Speaker of Lok Sabha - Appointed by the President
3. Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha - Elected by members of Lok Sabha
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Match the following: 1. Chief Election Commissioner - Elected by members of Rajya Sabha 2. Speaker of Lok Sabha - Appointed by the President 3. Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha - Elected by members of Lok Sabha Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- **Chief Election Commissioner** ' **Appointed by the President** (Article 324).
- **Speaker of Lok Sabha** ' **Elected by members of Lok Sabha** in its first sitting by simple majority.
- **Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha** ' **Elected by members of Rajya Sabha** from amongst themselves.
- These positions ensure the independence of constitutional bodies and the smooth functioning of Parliament.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part VIII & X: Union Territories and Scheduled Areas
questions.microTopic: UT, Scheduled Areas, Other Scheduled Areas
statementLines: The Eighth Schedule of Indian Constitution contains
questions.questionText: The Eighth Schedule of Indian Constitution contains

options.c: A list of 18 languages recognised by the Constitution
options.d: Number of States and Union Territories

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The **Eighth Schedule** of the Constitution lists the **languages recognised by the Constitution**.
- It originally contained **14 languages**, and over time, more were added through amendments:
- **Sindhi** was added in **1967** (21st Amendment).
- **Konkani, Manipuri, Nepali** were added in **1992** (71st Amendment).
- **Bodo, Santhali, Maithili, Dogri** were added in **2004** (92nd Amendment).
- As of now, the total number of languages is **22**.
- The Government is responsible for the development of these languages under **Article 351**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Sarkaria Commission has been set up to study
questions.questionText: Sarkaria Commission has been set up to study
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **Sarkaria Commission** was constituted in **1983** under the chairmanship of **Justice R.S. Sarkaria**.
- It was set up to review the **Centre"State relations** and suggest reforms to strengthen **cooperative federalism**.
- Key recommendations included:
- Establishing a permanent **Inter-State Council under Article 263**.
- Using **Article 356 (Presidents Rule)** only as a last resort.
- Promoting consultation with States before legislating on **Concurrent List subjects**.
- Reactivating **Zonal Councils** for better coordination.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
statementLines: It is often said that the key to the minds of the makers of our Constitution lies in
questions.questionText: It is often said that the key to the minds of the makers of our Constitution lies in
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **Preamble** embodies the **philosophy, values, and objectives** that guided the framers of the Constitution.
- It is based on the **Objectives Resolution** moved by **Jawaharlal Nehru in 1946**.
- The Supreme Court in the **Berubari case (**1960**)** observed that the Preamble is a **key to open the mind of the makers of the Constitution**.
- It sets out the ideals of justice, liberty, equality, fraternity, and defines the nature of the Indian State as **sovereign, socialist, secular, and democratic republic**.
- It also serves as an **interpretative tool** when there is ambiguity in constitutional provisions.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: The Jammu and Kashmir Resettlement Bill which has stirred up controversy deals with
questions.questionText: The Jammu and Kashmir Resettlement Bill which has stirred up controversy deals with

options.a: Reorganisation of territories between Jammu & Kashmir
options.b: Allowing people of Kashmiri origin who have migrated to Pakistan to come back to Jammu & Kashmir
options.c: Introduction of new land reforms in the State
options.d: Imposition of agricultural income tax in the State

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The **Jammu and Kashmir Resettlement Act, 1982** provided for the resettlement of persons of Kashmiri origin who migrated to Pakistan after March **1947**, allowing them to return and reclaim property in J&K.
- The Act became controversial due to security and citizenship concerns, as it could allow large numbers of Pakistanis (including potentially hostile elements) to enter the state.
- The Supreme Court stayed the implementation of the Act, and the matter remained unresolved for years.
- The Governor initially returned the Bill, but it was passed again without changes, leading to a Presidential Reference to the Supreme Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Which one of the following is not a piece of social legislation?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not a piece of social legislation?

options.a: The Immoral Offences and Girls Trafficking Act
options.b: The Maintenance of Internal Security Act

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- The **Maintenance of Internal Security Act (MISA), 1971** was a law giving the government extensive powers for **preventive detention**, search, and wiretapping, mainly used during the **Emergency (**1975**"77)**.
- It was aimed at **internal security and political control**, not at bringing social reform.
- The other Acts are social legislations:
- **Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956** " combats trafficking and exploitation.
- **Abolition of Untouchability (Offences) Act, 1955** " enforces **Article 17**.
- **Special Marriage Act, 1954** " provides legal framework for inter-religious and civil marriages.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The greatest hallmark of personal liberty is
questions.questionText: The greatest hallmark of personal liberty is
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.
- The writ of **Habeas Corpus** (a Latin term meaning "to have the body of") is considered the greatest bulwark of personal liberty.
- It is issued by the Supreme Court (Article 32) or High Courts (Article 226) to command a person who has detained another to produce the body of the detainee before the court.
- The court examines the legality of the detention. If found unlawful, the person is freed.
- It is the most powerful tool against **arbitrary or illegal arrest and detention**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
statementLines: The reorganisation of Indian States on a linguistic basis took place in
questions.questionText: The reorganisation of Indian States on a linguistic basis took place in
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- The **States Reorganisation Act, 1956** came into effect on **1 November 1956**, fundamentally redrawing the boundaries of India's states on **linguistic lines**.
- The reorganisation was based on the recommendations of the **Fazl Ali Commission (States Reorganisation Commission,**1953**)**.
- The demand for linguistic states gained immense momentum after the death of Potti Sreeramulu, which forced the creation of the first linguistic state, **Andhra State, in 1953**.
- The **1956** Act created **14 States and 6 Union Territories**, replacing the old Part A, B, C, and D classification.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
statementLines: The Nagaland State of India cannot be termed a State because it lacks
questions.questionText: The Nagaland State of India cannot be termed a State because it lacks

options.d: A High Court exclusively of its own

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.
- In political science, a "State" in the international sense requires four elements: population, territory, government, and **sovereignty**.
- **Nagaland** (which became the 16th Indian state in **1963**) possesses population, territory, and government, but **it lacks sovereignty**.
